applicationInfo.name: ABCA - Autonomous Background Coding Agents on AWS
applicationInfo.description:
### Summary
ABCA is a self-hosted platform on AWS that runs autonomous background coding agents. Users submit coding tasks via CLI, webhooks, or Slack, and agents autonomously clone repos, write code, run tests, and open pull requests in isolated MicroVM environments.

### Key objectives
- Enable fire-and-forget autonomous coding task execution in the cloud
- Provide security-by-default with isolated sandboxed compute and least-privilege access
- Support cross-session learning through tiered memory backed by AgentCore Memory
- Ensure reliability by driving every task to a terminal state regardless of agent failures
- Maintain cost efficiency with hybrid deterministic/agentic workflow design

### Use-cases
- Submit coding tasks that agents complete autonomously and open PRs for review
- Iterate on PR review feedback automatically via pr_iteration tasks
- Perform automated PR reviews via pr_review tasks
- Onboard repositories with per-repo configuration (model, compute, policies)
- Integrate with Slack and Linear for task submission and notifications

### Features
- Task orchestration with blueprint-based hybrid workflows (deterministic + agentic steps)
- Isolated MicroVM compute per task via Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Runtime or ECS
- Cedar-based policy enforcement for tool-call access control (HITL gates)
- Input screening via Bedrock Guardrails with fail-closed semantics
- CLI, REST API, Slack, Linear, and webhook input channels
- Per-repo memory system with provenance tagging and poisoning defenses
- DNS Firewall domain allowlisting and VPC-based network isolation

architecture.description:
### High Level Architecture

#### Application type
Serverless event-driven autonomous coding agent platform deployed on AWS (API + orchestrator + isolated compute)

#### Layers
- Input layer: REST API (API Gateway + Cognito + WAFv2), Slack integration, Linear integration, Webhook (HMAC)
- Orchestration layer: Lambda Durable Functions driving task state machine (admission, hydration, session mgmt, finalization)
- Compute layer: Isolated MicroVM sessions (Bedrock AgentCore Runtime / optional ECS Fargate) running Python agent with Claude Agent SDK
- Data layer: DynamoDB tables (tasks, events, approvals, concurrency, repos, webhooks), S3 (attachments, traces), Secrets Manager, AgentCore Memory
- Integration layer: GitHub (clone/push/PR via PAT), Amazon Bedrock (LLM inference), DNS Firewall (egress control)

#### Boundaries
- Internet to API Gateway (WAFv2 protected, Cognito JWT or HMAC auth)
- API Gateway Lambda handlers to Orchestrator Lambda (async invoke)
- Orchestrator to AgentCore Runtime (SigV4 invoke_agent_runtime across VPC boundary)
- AgentCore MicroVM to external services (NAT Gateway, DNS Firewall allowlist: GitHub, npm, PyPI, AWS)
- Agent to Bedrock Runtime (VPC endpoint, model invocation for code generation)

#### Technology components
- AWS CDK (TypeScript) - Infrastructure as Code, single stack deployment
- Python 3.x + Claude Agent SDK + FastAPI - Agent runtime in MicroVM container
- TypeScript + Node.js 24 - Lambda handlers, orchestrator, CLI
- Amazon Bedrock (Claude Sonnet 4/Haiku/Opus) - LLM inference via cross-region profiles
- DynamoDB - Task state, events, approvals, concurrency, repo config, webhooks
- Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Runtime - MicroVM isolation, session lifecycle (Firecracker)
- Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Memory - Cross-session semantic/episodic memory
- Cedar policy engine (WASM Lambda layer) - Tool-call access control and HITL gates

#### Deployment architecture
- Single AWS account, single CDK stack (backgroundagent-dev)
- VPC with private subnets (AgentCore Runtime), public subnets (NAT Gateway)
- VPC Endpoints: S3, DynamoDB, ECR, CloudWatch, Secrets Manager, Bedrock Runtime, STS
- DNS Firewall with domain allowlist (observation mode default)
- Single NAT Gateway for internet egress (configurable multi-AZ)

#### APIs

| Endpoint | Method | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| /v1/tasks | POST | Create coding task (new_task, pr_iteration, pr_review) |
| /v1/tasks | GET | List user tasks (paginated, filterable) |
| /v1/tasks/{id} | GET | Get task details with error classification |
| /v1/tasks/{id} | DELETE | Cancel task (stops session, releases concurrency) |
| /v1/tasks/{id}/events | GET | Task audit trail (state transitions, events) |
| /v1/tasks/{id}/approve | POST | Approve Cedar HITL gate |
| /v1/tasks/{id}/deny | POST | Deny Cedar HITL gate |
| /v1/webhooks/tasks | POST | Create task via HMAC webhook |

#### Security relevant components
- Amazon Cognito User Pool (JWT auth, no MFA in dev) + HMAC-SHA256 webhooks
- Bedrock Guardrails (PROMPT_ATTACK filter, fail-closed input screening at submission and hydration)
- Cedar policy engine (PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks, hard/soft deny, HITL approval gates)
- MicroVM session isolation (Firecracker, per-task compute/memory/filesystem)
- DNS Firewall domain allowlisting + Security Group TCP 443 only egress
- Output secret scanner (PostToolUse redaction of AWS keys, tokens, private keys)
- WAFv2 (CRS, known bad inputs, IP rate limit 1000/5min)

### Low Level Architecture
- API Gateway REST API (v1 stage) with Cognito authorizer and REQUEST authorizer for webhooks
- WAFv2 Web ACL with AWS Managed Rules (CRS, KnownBadInputs) and IP rate limiting
- Cognito User Pool (email sign-in, SRP/password auth flows, no MFA)
- Lambda handlers: CreateTask, GetTask, ListTasks, CancelTask, GetTaskEvents, ApproveTask, DenyTask, GetPending, NudgeTask, ConfirmUploads, GetTraceUrl, WebhookAuthorizer, WebhookCreateTask, GetPolicies
- TaskOrchestrator Lambda (Durable Functions, checkpoint/replay, 30s poll interval)
- AgentCore Runtime (Docker image from agent/Dockerfile, 8h max lifetime, /invocations + /ping endpoints)
- FastAPI server (port 8080) spawning background thread for pipeline execution
- Pipeline stages: setup_repo, assemble_prompt, run_agent (Claude Agent SDK), post_hooks (ensure_committed, ensure_pr, verify_build)
- DynamoDB Tables: TaskTable (GSI: UserStatus, Status, Idempotency), TaskEventsTable (DDB Streams to FanOut + ApprovalMetrics), TaskApprovalsTable, UserConcurrencyTable, RepoTable, WebhookTable, TaskNudgesTable, SlackInstallationTable, SlackUserMappingTable, LinearProjectMappingTable, LinearUserMappingTable
- S3 Buckets: AttachmentsBucket (versioned, 90-day lifecycle), TraceArtifactsBucket (7-day TTL)
- Secrets Manager: GitHub PAT, Slack signing/client secrets, webhook secrets (bgagent/webhook/*), Linear API token
- Bedrock Guardrail (InputGuardrail, PROMPT_ATTACK MEDIUM strength)
- Cedar WASM Lambda Layer for policy evaluation
- AgentCore Memory (semantic knowledge + episodic strategies, per-repo namespaces)
- SlackIntegration (commands, events, interactions, OAuth, notify via DDB Streams fan-out)
- LinearIntegration (webhook processor, project/user mapping tables)
- ConcurrencyReconciler (15-min schedule, drift correction)
- StrandedTaskReconciler (catches orphaned SUBMITTED/HYDRATING tasks)
- PendingUploadCleanup (30-min auto-cancel for unconfirmed uploads)
- TaskDashboard (CloudWatch dashboard for operational monitoring)
- VPC: Private subnets + NAT Gateway + VPC Endpoints + DNS Firewall rule group

dataflow.description:
### Human actors

- **Developer/Operator** - Submits coding tasks via CLI (bgagent), approves/denies Cedar HITL gates, reviews PRs
- **Slack User** - Submits tasks via /bgagent slash command, receives notifications in threads
- **Linear User** - Triggers tasks by applying labels to Linear issues
- **Platform Admin** - Deploys CDK stack, manages repo onboarding, configures Cedar policies

### External entities

- **GitHub** - Source code hosting (clone/push/PR), issue/PR context fetching, webhook events
- **Amazon Bedrock** - LLM inference (Claude models), Guardrails (prompt attack screening), AgentCore Runtime (MicroVM), AgentCore Memory
- **Slack API** - OAuth, slash commands, events, interactions, message posting
- **Linear API** - Webhook delivery, issue reactions, comment posting
- **Package registries** - npm, PyPI (egress from MicroVM for dependency installation)

### Processes

- **API Gateway + Lambda Handlers** - REST API request processing (create/get/list/cancel tasks, approve/deny gates, webhooks)
- **Task Orchestrator** - Lambda Durable Functions driving task state machine (admission, hydration, session start, poll, finalize)
- **Agent Runtime (MicroVM)** - FastAPI server in AgentCore MicroVM executing the coding pipeline (repo setup, prompt assembly, Claude SDK agent loop, post-hooks)
- **Cedar Policy Engine** - WASM Lambda layer evaluating tool-call permissions (PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks)
- **Fan-out Consumer** - DDB Streams Lambda routing task events to Slack, GitHub comments, and email channels
- **Reconcilers** - Scheduled Lambdas (ConcurrencyReconciler, StrandedTaskReconciler, PendingUploadCleanup)
- **Slack/Linear Integration Lambdas** - Command processing, event handling, OAuth, webhook verification

### Data stores

| Store | Type | High-Value (Y/N) |
|---|---|---|
| TaskTable (DynamoDB) | NoSQL - task state, results, metadata | Y |
| TaskEventsTable (DynamoDB + Streams) | NoSQL - audit trail, state transitions | Y |
| TaskApprovalsTable (DynamoDB) | NoSQL - Cedar HITL approval requests/decisions | Y |
| Secrets Manager | Secret store - GitHub PAT, Slack secrets, webhook secrets, Linear token | Y |
| AttachmentsBucket (S3) | Object store - user-uploaded files (versioned, 90-day lifecycle) | N |
| TraceArtifactsBucket (S3) | Object store - agent trajectory traces (7-day TTL) | N |
| AgentCore Memory | Managed semantic store - per-repo episodes and learnings | N |

### Trust boundaries

| Zone | Elements | Trust Level |
|---|---|---|
| Internet/Untrusted | Users, Slack, Linear, GitHub webhooks | Untrusted - all input screened |
| API Gateway (WAFv2) | REST endpoints, Cognito authorizer, HMAC authorizer | Perimeter - authenticated requests only |
| AWS Account (Lambda) | Orchestrator, Handlers, Cedar engine, Fan-out | Trusted compute - IAM-scoped |
| VPC Private Subnets (MicroVM) | AgentCore Runtime, Agent process, cloned repos | Isolated compute - per-task filesystem, DNS Firewall |
| External APIs | GitHub API, Bedrock API, package registries | Semi-trusted - egress-controlled via allowlist |

### Data flows

| Flow ID | Description | Source | Target | Trust Boundaries Crossed | Assets |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| DF-01 | User submits task via CLI/API | Developer | API Gateway | Internet to Perimeter | Task description, repo URL, Cognito JWT |
| DF-02 | Webhook task submission (HMAC) | External system | API Gateway | Internet to Perimeter | Task payload, HMAC signature |
| DF-03 | Slack slash command task creation | Slack User | Slack Integration Lambda | Internet to Perimeter | Command text, Slack signing secret |
| DF-04 | Linear label-triggered task | Linear API | Linear Webhook Lambda | Internet to Perimeter | Issue metadata, webhook signature |
| DF-05 | Orchestrator invokes AgentCore | Task Orchestrator | AgentCore Runtime | Lambda to VPC Private | Hydrated context, GitHub token, Cedar policies |
| DF-06 | Agent clones repo and pushes code | Agent Runtime | GitHub | VPC to Internet (NAT) | Git operations, GitHub PAT |
| DF-07 | Agent calls Bedrock for inference | Agent Runtime | Amazon Bedrock | VPC to Bedrock (VPC Endpoint) | Prompts, code context, model responses |
| DF-08 | Guardrail screens user input | Orchestrator Lambda | Bedrock Guardrails | Within AWS Account | User prompt, issue/PR content |
| DF-09 | Agent writes task state to DDB | Agent Runtime | TaskTable | VPC to DynamoDB (VPC Endpoint) | Task status, results, heartbeat |
| DF-10 | Agent writes memory episodes | Agent Runtime | AgentCore Memory | VPC to Bedrock AgentCore | Task outcomes, repo learnings |
| DF-11 | Fan-out posts to Slack/GitHub | Fan-out Consumer | Slack API / GitHub API | Lambda to Internet | Task events, PR URLs, status updates |
| DF-12 | User approves/denies HITL gate | Developer | API Gateway | Internet to Perimeter | Approval decision, scope, request_id |
| DF-13 | Orchestrator reads GitHub context | Task Orchestrator | GitHub API | Lambda to Internet | Issue/PR bodies, comments, diffs |
| DF-14 | Agent uploads trace artifacts | Agent Runtime | TraceArtifactsBucket | VPC to S3 (VPC Endpoint) | Compressed trajectory JSONL |
| DF-15 | Agent downloads attachments | Agent Runtime | AttachmentsBucket | VPC to S3 (VPC Endpoint) | User-uploaded files (integrity-verified) |

assumptions.content: The analyzed codebase represents the complete application as intended for deployment, with no significant external modules missing.

metadata.value: The repository contains CDK infrastructure, agent Python code, CLI TypeScript code, and comprehensive documentation. The monorepo structure (cdk, agent, cli, docs workspaces) appears complete.

assumptions.content: The application is deployed as a single-tenant platform within one AWS account owned by the operator.

metadata.value: Documentation references self-hosted deployment in customer AWS account. Multi-tenant is mentioned as requiring silo model for AgentCore Memory isolation, suggesting single-tenant is the default.

assumptions.content: The technology stack consists of AWS CDK (TypeScript) for infrastructure, Python for the agent runtime, and TypeScript for the CLI.

metadata.value: CDK uses TypeScript with Lambda handlers. Agent uses Python with Claude Agent SDK and FastAPI. CLI is TypeScript. Node >= 20.x. Documentation site uses Astro.

assumptions.content: The platform is experimental and not intended for production use without additional hardening.

metadata.value: README explicitly states experimental stability badge and disclaimer that it is for experimental and educational purposes only, not intended for direct production use.

assumptions.content: GitHub is the sole supported source code hosting platform for agent interactions (clone, push, PR).

metadata.value: All documentation and code reference GitHub exclusively. DNS Firewall allowlists github.com. PAT-based auth targets GitHub. No GitLab/Bitbucket support observed.

assumptions.content: Amazon Bedrock with Claude models is the sole LLM inference provider used by the agent runtime.

metadata.value: Architecture docs reference Bedrock Runtime VPC endpoints, Bedrock Guardrails, and Claude model variants (Sonnet, Haiku, Opus). No other LLM providers are referenced.

assumptions.content: Authentication relies on Amazon Cognito for CLI/API users and HMAC-SHA256 for webhook integrations.

metadata.value: Security doc confirms Cognito JWT for CLI/REST and HMAC-SHA256 for webhooks. MFA is noted as disabled. No federated identity providers are configured by default.

assumptions.content: The AgentCore Runtime is the primary compute backend; ECS Fargate is commented out and not actively deployed.

metadata.value: The ECS cluster construct import is commented out in agent.ts. The ECS strategy code exists but the CDK stack does not instantiate it. AgentCore is the only active compute path.

assumptions.content: DNS Firewall is deployed in observation mode (ALERT only, not BLOCK) based on default configuration.

metadata.value: The DnsFirewall construct is instantiated with observationMode: true. Non-allowlisted domains are logged but not blocked. Enforcement requires explicit operator opt-in.

assumptions.content: A single shared GitHub PAT is used for all repositories; per-repo scoped tokens are not yet implemented.

metadata.value: One Secrets Manager secret (GitHubTokenSecret) stores the PAT. The orchestrator passes it to all agent sessions regardless of repo. GitHub App integration is listed as planned in the roadmap.

assumptions.content: The orchestrator uses Lambda Durable Functions for checkpoint/replay, enabling hours-long task monitoring without compute charges.

metadata.value: Confirmed in ORCHESTRATOR.md and task-orchestrator.ts. The orchestrator polls every 30s via waitForCondition, suspending between polls. Execution duration supports up to 1 year.

assumptions.content: All DynamoDB tables use on-demand capacity mode with PITR enabled and 90-day TTL for task data.

metadata.value: SECURITY.md confirms PITR on all tables. Task retention defaults to 90 days (TASK_RETENTION_DAYS env var). On-demand mode inferred from absence of provisioned capacity in constructs.

assumptions.content: The agent container image must fit within 2GB (AgentCore hard limit) including OS, runtimes, and agent code.

metadata.value: COMPUTE.md documents the 2GB non-adjustable image limit. The Dockerfile builds from repo root to include contracts/constants.json. Repos exceeding this limit require ECS backend.

assumptions.content: Slack and Linear integrations are always deployed but require post-deploy secret population to become functional.

metadata.value: Both SlackIntegration and LinearIntegration constructs are unconditionally instantiated. Secrets (signing secret, client secret, API tokens) are created empty and populated after deployment.

assumptions.content: The TaskEventsTable DynamoDB Stream has exactly two consumers (FanOutConsumer and ApprovalMetricsPublisher); adding a third requires migration to Kinesis Data Streams.

metadata.value: Noted in the CDK stack comments and task-events-table.ts. DynamoDB Streams supports max 2 Lambda consumers per stream. A third consumer would require Kinesis Data Streams for DynamoDB.

assumptions.content: The GitHub PAT passed to the MicroVM has broad repo access; compromise of one session exposes all onboarded repositories.

metadata.value: A single GitHub PAT from Secrets Manager is injected into every agent session. The token is set as GITHUB_TOKEN env var inside the container. No per-repo scoping exists today.

assumptions.content: The orchestrator Lambda has network access to GitHub API for context hydration (issue/PR fetching) without VPC constraints.

metadata.value: Context hydration in the orchestrator Lambda fetches GitHub issues and PRs directly via HTTPS. Lambda runs outside the VPC (no VPC config observed on handler Lambdas), so DNS Firewall does not apply to these calls.

assumptions.content: DynamoDB Streams deliver task events to exactly one fan-out consumer which routes to Slack, GitHub, and email channels.

metadata.value: The FanOutConsumer Lambda processes TaskEventsTable DDB Stream records and dispatches to Slack (threaded messages), GitHub (edit-in-place comment), and email (stub). A second consumer handles approval metrics.

assumptions.content: The agent runtime communicates task progress back to the orchestrator exclusively via DynamoDB writes (no direct callback).

metadata.value: The orchestrator polls TaskTable every 30s via waitForCondition. The agent writes status, heartbeat, and terminal results to DynamoDB. There is no direct HTTP callback from agent to orchestrator.

assumptions.content: Cedar HITL approval polling occurs within the agent MicroVM process, blocking the agent turn until a decision or timeout.

metadata.value: The PreToolUse hook writes an approval row to TaskApprovalsTable, transitions task to AWAITING_APPROVAL, then polls the row every 2-5s from within the agent process. The user approves via the REST API which updates the row.

assumptions.content: Bedrock Guardrail screening is applied at two points: task submission (user prompt) and context hydration (when GitHub issue/PR content is present).

metadata.value: The screenWithGuardrail function is called during hydrateContext for PR tasks (always) and new_task (when issue content exists). Fail-closed: GuardrailScreeningError propagates and fails the task rather than allowing unscreened content.

assumptions.content: The output secret scanner (PostToolUse) catches common credential patterns but may miss novel or obfuscated secret formats.

metadata.value: The PostToolUse hook scans for AWS keys, tokens, and private keys. However, regex-based scanning cannot catch all credential formats (e.g., base64-encoded secrets, split across multiple outputs, or novel token formats). This affects the reliability of the exfiltration prevention control.

assumptions.content: The MicroVM IAM role has DynamoDB write access scoped to the specific task partition key, not broad table-level write access.

metadata.value: This assumption affects the blast radius of a compromised agent session. If IAM policies scope DynamoDB writes to the task's own partition key, a compromised agent cannot tamper with other tasks' records. If scoping is at the table level, cross-task tampering is possible. The exact IAM policy needs verification.

assumptions.content: Bedrock Guardrails PROMPT_ATTACK filter at MEDIUM strength provides reasonable but not comprehensive protection against prompt injection.

metadata.value: MEDIUM strength balances false positives (blocking legitimate coding tasks that discuss security) against false negatives (missing sophisticated injections). Indirect injections embedded in code, documentation, or issue comments are particularly challenging to detect as they resemble legitimate content.

assumptions.content: The Cedar policy engine correctly enforces tool-call restrictions and there are no bypass paths that skip PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks.

metadata.value: The threat model assumes Cedar enforcement is correctly implemented in the agent SDK integration. If there are code paths where tools can be invoked without triggering the Cedar hooks (e.g., direct subprocess calls, dynamic tool loading), the access control would be bypassed.

assumptions.content: WAFv2 rate limiting at 1000 requests per 5 minutes is the only API-level throttling; no per-user or per-IP adaptive rate limiting exists.

metadata.value: The WAFv2 configuration uses a fixed IP rate limit. This may be insufficient against distributed attacks or authenticated abuse. Per-user concurrency limits exist at the task level but not at the API request level.

assumptions.content: The StrandedTaskReconciler and ConcurrencyReconciler provide eventual consistency but leave windows of 15-30 minutes where orphaned resources may persist.

metadata.value: Reconcilers run on 15-minute schedules. During the interval between runs, stranded tasks consume concurrency slots and orphaned MicroVMs consume compute. An attacker could exploit this window for burst resource consumption.

mitigations.content: Enable MFA on the Cognito User Pool using TOTP software tokens. Configure the Cognito user pool to require MFA for all users (MFA enforcement = REQUIRED). Update the CLI authentication flow to support MFA challenge responses during SRP authentication.

metadata.value: Directly addresses the absence of MFA that enables credential stuffing. Cognito supports TOTP MFA natively. The CLI (TypeScript) needs to handle the SOFTWARE_TOKEN_MFA challenge in the auth flow. This is a low-effort, high-impact change that eliminates the primary authentication weakness.

mitigations.content: Implement adaptive rate limiting on Cognito authentication endpoints using a Lambda pre-authentication trigger that tracks failed attempts per email in DynamoDB with exponential backoff lockout (e.g., 5 failures = 15min lockout, 10 = 1hr).

metadata.value: Supplements MFA by providing defense-in-depth against credential attacks. WAFv2 rate limiting at 1000/5min is too permissive for auth endpoints. A Cognito pre-authentication Lambda trigger can enforce per-user lockouts independent of source IP, defeating distributed attacks.

mitigations.content: Implement automatic rotation of webhook secrets in Secrets Manager with a 30-day rotation schedule. Use a Lambda rotation function that updates both Secrets Manager and the WebhookTable with the new secret, supporting a grace period where both old and new secrets are valid during rotation.

metadata.value: Limits the window of exploitation for compromised webhook secrets. Secrets Manager native rotation with a custom Lambda rotator can handle the dual-secret validation period. Combined with CloudTrail monitoring of GetSecretValue calls, this reduces the risk of long-lived compromised credentials.

mitigations.content: Add IP allowlisting for webhook sources by configuring WAFv2 rules that restrict the /v1/webhooks/* path to known source IP ranges (e.g., CI/CD system IPs). Store allowed CIDRs in an IP set that can be updated without redeployment.

metadata.value: Even with a compromised HMAC secret, attackers cannot forge requests unless they originate from allowed IP ranges. WAFv2 IP sets support dynamic updates. This is particularly effective for webhook integrations where source IPs are predictable (e.g., GitHub webhook IPs, internal CI systems).

mitigations.content: Implement a secondary content screening layer using a dedicated prompt injection classifier model (separate from Bedrock Guardrails) that runs on GitHub issue/PR content before it enters the agent context. Use a fine-tuned classifier that specifically targets code-embedded injection patterns.

metadata.value: Bedrock Guardrails at MEDIUM strength is a single point of failure for prompt injection detection. A secondary classifier provides defense-in-depth. This can be a lightweight model (e.g., Haiku-based) that specifically looks for instruction-like patterns in code comments, issue bodies, and PR descriptions. The two-layer approach catches injections that either system alone would miss.

mitigations.content: Implement output validation on agent-generated code by running a post-generation diff analysis that flags suspicious patterns: new network calls to non-allowlisted domains, environment variable reads of sensitive vars, base64 encoding of secrets, or modifications to files outside the expected scope.

metadata.value: Even if prompt injection bypasses input screening, output validation can detect the effects. A PostToolUse hook analyzing git diffs before push can identify: (1) unexpected outbound network calls, (2) access to GITHUB_TOKEN or other env vars in new code, (3) modifications to CI/CD configs or security-sensitive files, (4) obfuscated code patterns. This catches the impact rather than the injection itself.

mitigations.content: Replace the shared GitHub PAT with GitHub App installation tokens scoped per-repository. Create a GitHub App, install it on target repos, and generate short-lived installation tokens (1hr expiry) at task hydration time, passing only the repo-specific token to the MicroVM.

metadata.value: This is the highest-impact mitigation for the blast radius problem. GitHub App installation tokens are scoped to specific repositories and expire in 1 hour. The orchestrator generates a token for only the target repo at hydration time. Even if the MicroVM is fully compromised, the attacker can only access the single target repository. This is already on the roadmap but should be prioritized.

mitigations.content: As an interim measure before GitHub App migration, create multiple fine-grained PATs each scoped to a single repository or small group of related repos. Map repos to their specific PAT in the RepoTable and inject only the matching token into each MicroVM session.

metadata.value: GitHub fine-grained PATs (beta) support per-repo scoping. While less elegant than GitHub Apps, this can be implemented quickly by adding a tokenSecretArn field to the RepoTable and modifying the orchestrator to look up the repo-specific secret. Reduces blast radius from all-repos to single-repo immediately.

mitigations.content: Switch DNS Firewall from observation mode to enforcement mode (BLOCK action for non-allowlisted domains). Add explicit allowlist entries for required domains (github.com, registry.npmjs.org, pypi.org, AWS endpoints) and block all others with a fallback BLOCK rule.

metadata.value: The single most impactful change for preventing data exfiltration. Changing observationMode from true to false in the DnsFirewall construct activates blocking. This prevents compromised agents from contacting attacker-controlled domains. Requires testing to ensure the allowlist is complete (check CloudWatch DNS Firewall ALERT logs for legitimate domains being accessed before switching to BLOCK).

mitigations.content: Implement VPC Flow Logs analysis with automated alerting for unusual egress patterns from MicroVM subnets. Create CloudWatch metric filters that trigger alarms when: (1) data transfer exceeds baseline per session, (2) connections to IPs not resolving to allowlisted domains, (3) sustained high-bandwidth transfers.

metadata.value: Provides visibility into exfiltration attempts even if DNS Firewall is bypassed (e.g., via direct IP connections or DNS-over-HTTPS). VPC Flow Logs capture all network traffic metadata. Baseline normal agent traffic volume (clone, push, npm install) and alert on anomalies. This is the detective control that complements DNS Firewall enforcement.

mitigations.content: Strip the GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable from the MicroVM process after the initial git clone and remote configuration is complete. Use a wrapper script that performs git credential setup (git credential store), then unsets the environment variable before launching the agent process.

metadata.value: Reduces the window where the PAT is accessible in memory. After git clone and remote setup, the credential is stored in git's credential helper (file-based, accessible only for git operations). The env var is cleared so casual reads of /proc/self/environ or os.environ cannot retrieve it. Does not prevent a determined attacker from reading the credential file but raises the bar significantly.

mitigations.content: Increase Bedrock Guardrails PROMPT_ATTACK filter to HIGH strength for content sourced from untrusted origins (GitHub issues, PR comments, Linear issues). Maintain MEDIUM for user-submitted task descriptions from authenticated users to reduce false positives on legitimate prompts.

metadata.value: Differentiate screening strength based on trust level of the content source. Authenticated user prompts (DF-01) are semi-trusted and can use MEDIUM. Third-party content fetched from GitHub (DF-13) or Linear (DF-04) is untrusted and should use HIGH. This requires modifying the screenWithGuardrail calls to accept a strength parameter based on content origin.

mitigations.content: Implement memory write validation for AgentCore Memory that requires provenance verification before storing episodes. Each memory write must include: task ID, authenticated user who initiated the task, timestamp, and a content hash. Implement a review queue for memories from tasks that triggered any security alerts.

metadata.value: Addresses memory poisoning by ensuring all stored memories have verifiable provenance. If a task triggered Guardrail alerts (even non-blocking ones), security anomalies, or unusual tool-call patterns, its memory writes should be quarantined for human review rather than immediately available to future sessions. This prevents a single compromised session from persistently affecting future tasks.

mitigations.content: Implement memory decay and confidence scoring for AgentCore Memory entries. Memories from tasks with clean execution (no security alerts, successful PR merge) receive high confidence. Memories from flagged tasks receive low confidence. Low-confidence memories are excluded from retrieval after 7 days if not validated.

metadata.value: Limits the persistence of poisoned memories. Even if a malicious memory is stored, its influence is time-bounded and weighted by the trustworthiness of its source task. Combined with provenance tagging, this creates a self-healing memory system where poisoned entries naturally expire unless validated by successful subsequent tasks.

mitigations.content: Disable npm/pip postinstall script execution during dependency installation in the MicroVM. Configure npm with --ignore-scripts flag and pip with --no-build-isolation --no-deps for initial install, then selectively run build steps only for known-safe packages defined in a per-repo allowlist.

metadata.value: Directly prevents arbitrary code execution via malicious postinstall hooks. The --ignore-scripts flag for npm and equivalent pip flags prevent lifecycle scripts from running. A per-repo allowlist in the RepoTable can specify packages that legitimately need build scripts (e.g., native addons). This breaks the primary attack vector for malicious repo content executing code during setup_repo.

mitigations.content: Implement a pre-clone repository validation step that checks the target repo against a denylist of known-malicious repos and validates that the repo belongs to an organization/owner explicitly registered in the RepoTable. Reject tasks targeting unregistered repositories.

metadata.value: Prevents attackers from targeting arbitrary repositories not explicitly onboarded. The RepoTable already stores per-repo configuration. Adding a strict validation that the task's target repo must exist in RepoTable with status=active prevents an attacker from pointing the agent at a malicious repo they control. This is a simple check in the admission/hydration phase.

mitigations.content: Implement lockfile integrity verification during setup_repo. Before running dependency installation, verify that package-lock.json/yarn.lock/poetry.lock exists and matches expected hashes stored in the RepoTable. Alert and fail the task if lockfiles have been tampered with or are missing.

metadata.value: Lockfiles pin exact dependency versions and prevent dependency confusion attacks. Verifying lockfile integrity before installation ensures the agent installs exactly the dependencies the repo maintainers intended. Combined with --ignore-scripts, this provides defense-in-depth against supply chain attacks during the setup phase.

mitigations.content: Implement per-session Bedrock inference call budgets enforced via a token counter in the agent runtime. Set a maximum token budget (input + output) per task based on task type (e.g., 500K for new_task, 200K for pr_review). Terminate the session when budget is exceeded.

metadata.value: Limits the blast radius of a compromised agent making excessive Bedrock calls. The agent runtime tracks cumulative token usage and self-terminates when the budget is exceeded. This is enforced in the agent process itself and backed by CloudWatch metrics for alerting. Prevents a single session from consuming the entire account's Bedrock quota.

mitigations.content: Implement Secrets Manager access logging and anomaly detection via CloudTrail. Create EventBridge rules that trigger on GetSecretValue API calls for high-value secrets (GitHub PAT, webhook secrets). Alert on access from unexpected IAM principals or at unusual times.

metadata.value: Provides visibility into secret access patterns. Normal access is from specific Lambda execution roles during task processing. Any access from IAM users, roles not in the expected set, or at unusual volumes indicates potential compromise. CloudTrail data events for Secrets Manager combined with EventBridge rules enable near-real-time alerting.

mitigations.content: Implement per-user task submission rate limiting at the application layer (beyond WAFv2). Add a sliding window counter in DynamoDB that limits each authenticated user to a configurable maximum tasks per hour (e.g., 10 new_task, 20 pr_iteration). Apply stricter limits to webhook-sourced tasks.

metadata.value: WAFv2 rate limiting is IP-based and does not prevent authenticated abuse. Application-layer rate limiting per user identity prevents a compromised account or leaked webhook from submitting unlimited tasks. The UserConcurrencyTable already tracks concurrent tasks; extending it with a submission rate counter is straightforward.

mitigations.content: Scope the MicroVM IAM role's DynamoDB write permissions to the specific task partition key using IAM policy conditions (dynamodb:LeadingKeys). Ensure the agent can only write to its own task record and events, not other tasks' partitions.

metadata.value: Prevents a compromised agent from tampering with other tasks' DynamoDB records. Use IAM condition keys: dynamodb:LeadingKeys with the task ID passed as a session tag or environment variable. The orchestrator sets the task ID when creating the session, and the IAM policy restricts writes to that partition key only.

mitigations.content: Implement Cedar HITL approval request enrichment that includes: full tool call parameters, diff preview of proposed changes, risk score based on operation type, and historical context of what the task has done so far. Present this in a structured format that resists social engineering.

metadata.value: Addresses approval fatigue and social engineering of HITL gates. Rich context in approval requests helps reviewers make informed decisions. A risk score (e.g., HIGH for force-push to main, LOW for creating a feature branch) draws attention to dangerous operations. Structured presentation prevents the agent from framing requests misleadingly.

mitigations.content: Implement independent agent action logging via CloudWatch Logs from the MicroVM sidecar process (not the agent itself). Capture all system calls, network connections, file modifications, and process executions in a tamper-resistant log stream that the agent process cannot modify.

metadata.value: Addresses the falsified trace artifact threat. A sidecar process (or kernel-level audit logging) running alongside the agent captures ground-truth actions independent of what the agent reports in its trajectory. Logs are streamed to CloudWatch in real-time, so even if the agent terminates abnormally, the audit trail is preserved. The agent process should not have IAM permissions to modify these log streams.

mitigations.content: Implement Slack channel restrictions for the bot integration: limit task submission to specific private channels with controlled membership. Configure the SlackUserMappingTable to require explicit user registration before Slack users can submit tasks.

metadata.value: Reduces the attack surface from compromised Slack accounts. Even if a Slack workspace is compromised, the attacker must be in the specific authorized channel AND have an explicit mapping in SlackUserMappingTable. Unmapped Slack users receive an error when attempting to use /bgagent. This limits blast radius to explicitly authorized users only.

mitigations.content: Implement automatic HITL approval timeout with deny-by-default. If no human responds to a Cedar HITL approval request within a configurable timeout (e.g., 30 minutes), automatically deny the request rather than leaving it pending indefinitely. Add a maximum pending approvals limit per user.

metadata.value: Addresses approval queue flooding. A maximum of N pending approvals per user (e.g., 5) prevents an attacker from generating unlimited approval requests. Combined with deny-by-default timeout, this ensures the approval queue cannot be weaponized for fatigue attacks. Tasks exceeding the pending limit are automatically failed.

mitigations.content: Sanitize notification content in the fan-out consumer by stripping URLs, code blocks, and markdown formatting from task event data before posting to Slack or GitHub. Only include pre-defined structured fields (task ID, status, repo name, PR number) with links generated server-side from known templates.

metadata.value: Prevents notification-based phishing by ensuring the fan-out consumer only emits controlled content. User-supplied task descriptions and error messages are truncated and sanitized. Links are constructed from known URL templates (e.g., github.com/org/repo/pull/N) rather than passed through from task data. This prevents a compromised task from injecting malicious links into notifications.

mitigations.content: Restrict Linear integration task triggers to a configurable set of authorized Linear team members. Validate that the user who applied the trigger label is in an allowlist stored in LinearUserMappingTable before creating the task. Reject label applications from unauthorized users.

metadata.value: Prevents any Linear workspace member from triggering tasks. The webhook payload includes the actor who applied the label. Validating this against an explicit allowlist ensures only authorized team members can trigger autonomous coding tasks via Linear, even if other workspace members can edit issues.

threats.prerequisites: with knowledge of a valid user's email address
threats.threatAction: exploit the absence of MFA on the Cognito User Pool to perform credential stuffing or password spraying attacks against the authentication endpoint
threats.threatImpact: unauthorized access to the platform allowing the attacker to submit malicious coding tasks, view task results, and access repository information
threats.statement: An internet-based actor with knowledge of a valid user's email address can exploit the absence of MFA on the Cognito User Pool to perform credential stuffing or password spraying attacks against the authentication endpoint, which leads to unauthorized access to the platform allowing the attacker to submit malicious coding tasks, view task results, and access repository information, resulting in reduced confidentiality, integrity of Cognito User Pool, TaskTable, GitHub repositories.

metadata.value: Cognito is configured without MFA (explicitly noted in security docs). SRP/password auth flows are enabled. WAFv2 rate limiting (1000/5min) provides some protection but may not be sufficient against distributed attacks. Successful authentication grants full API access including task creation which triggers autonomous code execution.

threats.prerequisites: who has compromised a webhook secret from Secrets Manager (bgagent/webhook/*)
threats.threatAction: forge valid HMAC-SHA256 signatures to submit arbitrary task payloads via the webhook endpoint
threats.threatImpact: execution of unauthorized coding tasks against any onboarded repository, potentially injecting malicious code into production codebases
threats.statement: An internet-based actor who has compromised a webhook secret from Secrets Manager (bgagent/webhook/*) can forge valid HMAC-SHA256 signatures to submit arbitrary task payloads via the webhook endpoint, which leads to execution of unauthorized coding tasks against any onboarded repository, potentially injecting malicious code into production codebases, resulting in reduced integrity, confidentiality of GitHub repositories, TaskTable, agent compute resources.

metadata.value: Webhook secrets are stored in Secrets Manager but if leaked (e.g., via misconfigured IAM, insider threat, or log exposure), an attacker can submit tasks that appear legitimate. The shared GitHub PAT means any submitted task can target any onboarded repo.

threats.prerequisites: with access to a GitHub repository that is onboarded to the platform
threats.threatAction: craft a prompt injection payload within a GitHub issue body or PR description that bypasses Bedrock Guardrails and manipulates the agent into executing unintended actions
threats.threatImpact: the agent performing unauthorized operations such as exfiltrating secrets, modifying unrelated files, or pushing malicious code to other branches
threats.statement: A malicious actor with access to a GitHub repository that is onboarded to the platform can craft a prompt injection payload within a GitHub issue body or PR description that bypasses Bedrock Guardrails and manipulates the agent into executing unintended actions, which leads to the agent performing unauthorized operations such as exfiltrating secrets, modifying unrelated files, or pushing malicious code to other branches, resulting in reduced integrity, confidentiality of GitHub repositories, GitHub PAT, agent runtime.

metadata.value: The orchestrator fetches GitHub issue/PR content (DF-13) and passes it to the agent. While Bedrock Guardrails screen for PROMPT_ATTACK at MEDIUM strength, sophisticated indirect prompt injections embedded in code comments, issue descriptions, or PR review comments may evade detection. The agent has the shared GitHub PAT and can push to any onboarded repo.

threats.prerequisites: with access to the shared GitHub PAT injected as GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable
threats.threatAction: use the PAT to access, clone, or push to any repository the token has permissions for, beyond the intended target repository
threats.threatImpact: unauthorized access to source code across all onboarded repositories, potential code injection into unrelated projects
threats.statement: A compromised agent session with access to the shared GitHub PAT injected as GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable can use the PAT to access, clone, or push to any repository the token has permissions for, beyond the intended target repository, which leads to unauthorized access to source code across all onboarded repositories, potential code injection into unrelated projects, resulting in reduced confidentiality, integrity of GitHub repositories, GitHub PAT, source code.

metadata.value: This is a critical blast radius issue. A single shared PAT with broad repo access is injected into every MicroVM session. If an agent is manipulated (via prompt injection or malicious repo content), it can access all repos the PAT covers. Per-repo scoped tokens are listed as a roadmap item but not yet implemented.

threats.prerequisites: who can submit tasks or influence repository content
threats.threatAction: exploit the DNS Firewall observation-only mode to exfiltrate data from the MicroVM to arbitrary external domains via HTTPS
threats.threatImpact: exfiltration of source code, secrets, or task context from the isolated compute environment to attacker-controlled infrastructure
threats.statement: A malicious actor who can submit tasks or influence repository content can exploit the DNS Firewall observation-only mode to exfiltrate data from the MicroVM to arbitrary external domains via HTTPS, which leads to exfiltration of source code, secrets, or task context from the isolated compute environment to attacker-controlled infrastructure, resulting in reduced confidentiality of source code, GitHub PAT, task context data.

metadata.value: DNS Firewall is deployed in observation mode (ALERT only, not BLOCK) by default. The agent MicroVM has NAT Gateway egress. While Security Groups restrict to TCP 443, any HTTPS endpoint is reachable. An attacker who manipulates the agent (via prompt injection or malicious dependencies) can exfiltrate data to any domain.

threats.prerequisites: with the ability to publish packages to npm or PyPI
threats.threatAction: perform a dependency confusion or typosquatting attack by publishing a malicious package that the agent installs during repository setup
threats.threatImpact: arbitrary code execution within the agent MicroVM, potentially leading to credential theft, data exfiltration, or code tampering
threats.statement: A malicious actor with the ability to publish packages to npm or PyPI can perform a dependency confusion or typosquatting attack by publishing a malicious package that the agent installs during repository setup, which leads to arbitrary code execution within the agent MicroVM, potentially leading to credential theft, data exfiltration, or code tampering, resulting in reduced integrity, confidentiality of agent runtime, GitHub PAT, source code.

metadata.value: The agent MicroVM has egress access to npm and PyPI (listed in DNS Firewall allowlist). During setup_repo, the agent installs dependencies. A supply chain attack via malicious packages would execute within the MicroVM which has the GitHub PAT and network access. MicroVM isolation limits blast radius to the single session but the PAT provides cross-repo access.

threats.prerequisites: with IAM permissions to read Secrets Manager secrets
threats.threatAction: retrieve the shared GitHub PAT, Slack secrets, or webhook secrets directly from Secrets Manager
threats.threatImpact: full access to all onboarded GitHub repositories, ability to impersonate the platform on Slack, and ability to forge webhook requests
threats.statement: An internal actor with IAM permissions to read Secrets Manager secrets can retrieve the shared GitHub PAT, Slack secrets, or webhook secrets directly from Secrets Manager, which leads to full access to all onboarded GitHub repositories, ability to impersonate the platform on Slack, and ability to forge webhook requests, resulting in reduced confidentiality, integrity of Secrets Manager, GitHub PAT, Slack secrets, webhook secrets.

metadata.value: Single-tenant deployment means AWS account administrators have implicit access to all secrets. The GitHub PAT is particularly high-value as it provides access to all onboarded repos. No secret rotation policy is documented. Multiple Lambda roles require Secrets Manager read access.

threats.prerequisites: with a network path to the API Gateway
threats.threatAction: submit a large volume of task creation requests that each trigger agent sessions consuming compute, DynamoDB writes, and Bedrock inference
threats.threatImpact: resource exhaustion causing denial of service for legitimate users and significant unexpected AWS costs
threats.statement: An internet-based actor with a network path to the API Gateway can submit a large volume of task creation requests that each trigger agent sessions consuming compute, DynamoDB writes, and Bedrock inference, which leads to resource exhaustion causing denial of service for legitimate users and significant unexpected AWS costs, resulting in reduced availability of AgentCore Runtime, DynamoDB tables, Bedrock inference quota, AWS billing.

metadata.value: While WAFv2 rate limits at 1000 requests per 5 minutes and UserConcurrencyTable enforces per-user limits, each task triggers expensive downstream operations (8h max MicroVM, Bedrock inference). A compromised account or leaked webhook secret could submit many tasks. The ConcurrencyReconciler runs only every 15 minutes, leaving a window for burst abuse.

threats.prerequisites: who controls content in a GitHub issue or PR that triggers a task
threats.threatAction: embed malicious instructions in code comments or PR review feedback that the agent interprets as legitimate coding instructions
threats.threatImpact: the agent introducing backdoors, vulnerabilities, or data exfiltration code into the target repository's pull request
threats.statement: A malicious actor who controls content in a GitHub issue or PR that triggers a task can embed malicious instructions in code comments or PR review feedback that the agent interprets as legitimate coding instructions, which leads to the agent introducing backdoors, vulnerabilities, or data exfiltration code into the target repository's pull request, resulting in reduced integrity of GitHub repositories, pull requests, source code integrity.

metadata.value: Indirect prompt injection via repository content is a key risk for autonomous coding agents. The Guardrail screens at MEDIUM strength which balances false positives vs security. Sophisticated injections disguised as legitimate code patterns or documentation may pass screening. The pr_iteration task type specifically processes PR review comments which are user-controlled.

threats.prerequisites: running within the MicroVM with DynamoDB write access via VPC endpoint
threats.threatAction: tamper with task state records in DynamoDB to mark malicious tasks as successful or manipulate the orchestrator's polling logic
threats.threatImpact: corrupted task audit trail, orchestrator making incorrect decisions based on falsified state, and potential bypass of finalization checks
threats.statement: A compromised agent process running within the MicroVM with DynamoDB write access via VPC endpoint can tamper with task state records in DynamoDB to mark malicious tasks as successful or manipulate the orchestrator's polling logic, which leads to corrupted task audit trail, orchestrator making incorrect decisions based on falsified state, and potential bypass of finalization checks, resulting in reduced integrity of TaskTable, TaskEventsTable, task audit trail.

metadata.value: The agent runtime writes directly to DynamoDB (task status, heartbeat, results). If the agent process is compromised, it could write arbitrary data to the task record. The orchestrator trusts DynamoDB state for decision-making. IAM scoping should limit writes to the specific task partition key but this needs verification.

threats.prerequisites: who can influence the content stored in AgentCore Memory for a repository
threats.threatAction: poison the semantic memory store with misleading learnings that cause future agent sessions to produce vulnerable or malicious code
threats.threatImpact: persistent compromise affecting all future tasks for the poisoned repository, with the agent repeatedly introducing vulnerabilities based on corrupted memory
threats.statement: A malicious actor who can influence the content stored in AgentCore Memory for a repository can poison the semantic memory store with misleading learnings that cause future agent sessions to produce vulnerable or malicious code, which leads to persistent compromise affecting all future tasks for the poisoned repository, with the agent repeatedly introducing vulnerabilities based on corrupted memory, resulting in reduced integrity of AgentCore Memory, future task outputs, source code.

metadata.value: The agent writes episodic memories and learnings to AgentCore Memory (DF-10). If an attacker can manipulate what gets stored (via prompt injection in a single session), those poisoned memories persist and influence all future sessions for that repo namespace. The documentation mentions provenance tagging and poisoning defenses but effectiveness depends on implementation.

threats.prerequisites: who has compromised a Slack workspace where the bot is installed
threats.threatAction: submit malicious coding tasks via the /bgagent slash command impersonating a legitimate Slack user
threats.threatImpact: unauthorized task execution against onboarded repositories with the attacker's instructions being processed as legitimate user requests
threats.statement: An internet-based actor who has compromised a Slack workspace where the bot is installed can submit malicious coding tasks via the /bgagent slash command impersonating a legitimate Slack user, which leads to unauthorized task execution against onboarded repositories with the attacker's instructions being processed as legitimate user requests, resulting in reduced integrity, confidentiality of GitHub repositories, agent compute resources, task integrity.

metadata.value: Slack signing secret verification authenticates that requests come from Slack, but does not prevent a compromised Slack account from submitting tasks. The SlackUserMappingTable maps Slack users to platform users. If a Slack account is compromised, the attacker inherits that user's task submission capabilities.

threats.prerequisites: with access to submit tasks to the platform
threats.threatAction: craft misleading task descriptions that trigger Cedar HITL approval requests framed to trick the human reviewer into approving dangerous operations
threats.threatImpact: human approver unknowingly authorizing destructive or exfiltration operations such as force-pushing to main branch or accessing sensitive files
threats.statement: A malicious actor with access to submit tasks to the platform can craft misleading task descriptions that trigger Cedar HITL approval requests framed to trick the human reviewer into approving dangerous operations, which leads to human approver unknowingly authorizing destructive or exfiltration operations such as force-pushing to main branch or accessing sensitive files, resulting in reduced integrity of GitHub repositories, Cedar HITL approval process.

metadata.value: Cedar HITL gates are a key security control but their effectiveness depends on the human reviewer understanding the full context of what they are approving. If the approval request metadata is insufficient or the agent has been manipulated to present misleading justifications, the human may approve dangerous operations.

threats.prerequisites: in a man-in-the-middle position between the orchestrator Lambda and GitHub API
threats.threatAction: intercept and modify GitHub issue/PR content during context hydration to inject malicious instructions into the task context
threats.threatImpact: the agent receiving tampered context containing prompt injection payloads not present in the original GitHub content
threats.statement: An internet-based actor in a man-in-the-middle position between the orchestrator Lambda and GitHub API can intercept and modify GitHub issue/PR content during context hydration to inject malicious instructions into the task context, which leads to the agent receiving tampered context containing prompt injection payloads not present in the original GitHub content, resulting in reduced integrity of task context, agent behavior, GitHub repositories.

metadata.value: The orchestrator Lambda fetches GitHub content directly over the internet (not through VPC/VPC endpoint). While HTTPS provides transport encryption, certificate pinning is unlikely. The Lambda runs outside the VPC so DNS Firewall does not apply. A compromised DNS or BGP hijack could redirect GitHub API calls.

threats.prerequisites: who has gained access to the fan-out consumer Lambda execution context
threats.threatAction: manipulate notification messages sent to Slack and GitHub to deliver phishing links or misleading status updates to developers
threats.threatImpact: developers trusting fraudulent notifications and clicking malicious links, approving compromised PRs, or taking incorrect actions
threats.statement: A malicious actor who has gained access to the fan-out consumer Lambda execution context can manipulate notification messages sent to Slack and GitHub to deliver phishing links or misleading status updates to developers, which leads to developers trusting fraudulent notifications and clicking malicious links, approving compromised PRs, or taking incorrect actions, resulting in reduced integrity, confidentiality of Slack notifications, GitHub comments, developer trust.

metadata.value: The fan-out consumer processes DDB Stream events and posts to Slack and GitHub. If the Lambda is compromised or if task event data is tampered with in DynamoDB, the notifications could contain malicious content. Developers may trust these automated messages implicitly since they come from the platform's bot identity.

threats.prerequisites: running in a MicroVM with Bedrock VPC endpoint access
threats.threatAction: make excessive LLM inference calls to Amazon Bedrock using the session's IAM credentials, consuming model invocation quotas
threats.threatImpact: denial of service for other agent sessions due to throttled Bedrock quotas and unexpected financial charges to the AWS account
threats.statement: A compromised agent process running in a MicroVM with Bedrock VPC endpoint access can make excessive LLM inference calls to Amazon Bedrock using the session's IAM credentials, consuming model invocation quotas, which leads to denial of service for other agent sessions due to throttled Bedrock quotas and unexpected financial charges to the AWS account, resulting in reduced availability of Bedrock inference quota, AWS billing, other agent sessions.

metadata.value: The agent MicroVM has IAM credentials for Bedrock model invocation via VPC endpoint. A compromised agent could make rapid inference calls. Bedrock quotas are account-level, so one abusive session could exhaust quotas for all concurrent sessions. The 8-hour max session lifetime provides an upper bound on abuse duration.

threats.prerequisites: who can submit a task with a crafted repository URL
threats.threatAction: specify a repository containing malicious build scripts or package.json postinstall hooks that execute during the agent's setup_repo phase
threats.threatImpact: arbitrary code execution in the MicroVM during dependency installation, enabling credential theft or lateral movement via the GitHub PAT
threats.statement: A malicious actor who can submit a task with a crafted repository URL can specify a repository containing malicious build scripts or package.json postinstall hooks that execute during the agent's setup_repo phase, which leads to arbitrary code execution in the MicroVM during dependency installation, enabling credential theft or lateral movement via the GitHub PAT, resulting in reduced integrity, confidentiality of agent runtime, GitHub PAT, other repositories.

metadata.value: The agent clones the target repo and runs setup steps (npm install, pip install, etc.) which can execute arbitrary scripts defined in package.json postinstall hooks, Makefiles, or setup.py. While the MicroVM provides isolation, the shared GitHub PAT is available as an environment variable and can be exfiltrated if DNS Firewall is in observation mode.

threats.prerequisites: who can observe task event notifications in Slack channels or GitHub comments
threats.threatAction: gather sensitive information about repository structure, task descriptions, and internal system behavior from notification content
threats.threatImpact: information disclosure enabling reconnaissance for more targeted attacks against the platform or the repositories it manages
threats.statement: An internet-based actor who can observe task event notifications in Slack channels or GitHub comments can gather sensitive information about repository structure, task descriptions, and internal system behavior from notification content, which leads to information disclosure enabling reconnaissance for more targeted attacks against the platform or the repositories it manages, resulting in reduced confidentiality of task metadata, repository information, system internals.

metadata.value: Fan-out notifications to Slack and GitHub may contain task descriptions, error stack traces, PR URLs, and status details. If Slack channels are not properly restricted or GitHub repos are public, this information could be accessible to unauthorized parties. Error messages may reveal internal architecture details.

threats.prerequisites: with access to the Linear workspace integrated with the platform
threats.threatAction: apply the trigger label to a Linear issue containing prompt injection payloads in the issue title or description to initiate a malicious task
threats.threatImpact: autonomous execution of attacker-controlled instructions by the coding agent against the mapped repository
threats.statement: A malicious actor with access to the Linear workspace integrated with the platform can apply the trigger label to a Linear issue containing prompt injection payloads in the issue title or description to initiate a malicious task, which leads to autonomous execution of attacker-controlled instructions by the coding agent against the mapped repository, resulting in reduced integrity of GitHub repositories, agent runtime, task integrity.

metadata.value: Linear integration triggers tasks when labels are applied to issues. Any Linear workspace member who can apply labels can trigger task execution. The issue content becomes the task description and is processed by the agent. While Guardrails screen the content, this is another indirect injection vector.

threats.prerequisites: who can trigger many concurrent tasks that enter AWAITING_APPROVAL state
threats.threatAction: flood the Cedar HITL approval queue with requests, overwhelming the human reviewer and causing approval fatigue
threats.threatImpact: approval fatigue leading to dangerous operations being approved without proper review, or legitimate tasks timing out
threats.statement: An internet-based actor who can trigger many concurrent tasks that enter AWAITING_APPROVAL state can flood the Cedar HITL approval queue with requests, overwhelming the human reviewer and causing approval fatigue, which leads to approval fatigue leading to dangerous operations being approved without proper review, or legitimate tasks timing out, resulting in reduced integrity, availability of Cedar HITL approval process, task execution, security controls.

metadata.value: The HITL approval mechanism relies on human attention as a security control. If an attacker can generate many approval requests (via multiple tasks or tasks that trigger many tool calls requiring approval), the human reviewer may experience fatigue and approve requests without careful review. This is a social engineering attack against the security control itself.

threats.prerequisites: with write access to the TraceArtifactsBucket via S3 VPC endpoint
threats.threatAction: write falsified or incomplete trace artifacts that conceal malicious actions taken during the agent session
threats.threatImpact: inability to perform forensic analysis of compromised sessions, loss of audit trail for security investigations
threats.statement: A compromised agent process with write access to the TraceArtifactsBucket via S3 VPC endpoint can write falsified or incomplete trace artifacts that conceal malicious actions taken during the agent session, which leads to inability to perform forensic analysis of compromised sessions, loss of audit trail for security investigations, resulting in reduced integrity of TraceArtifactsBucket, audit trail, forensic capability.

metadata.value: The agent uploads its own trace artifacts (compressed trajectory JSONL). A compromised agent could omit or falsify trace entries to hide malicious actions. The 7-day TTL on the bucket means evidence is automatically deleted. Without independent logging of agent actions, the trace is the primary forensic record.
